## Binary Search Tree (BST) Insertion — Time Complexity

**Average case:** O(log n) — each comparison eliminates roughly half the remaining tree.

**Worst case:** O(n) — occurs when insertions arrive in sorted order, degenerating the tree into a linked list.

### BST Insert Pseudocode

```
function BST_Insert(root, key):
    if root is NULL:
        return new Node(key)
    if key < root.key:
        root.left = BST_Insert(root.left, key)
    else if key > root.key:
        root.right = BST_Insert(root.right, key)
    return root
```

---

## Red-Black Tree: How It Maintains Balance

A red-black tree is a self-balancing BST that enforces **five invariants**:

1. Every node is either **red** or **black**.
2. The **root** is always black.
3. Every **NULL leaf** (sentinel) is black.
4. A **red node** cannot have a red child (no two consecutive reds).
5. Every path from a node to its descendant NULLs has the **same number of black nodes** (black-height).

These properties guarantee the tree height is at most **2·log₂(n+1)**, keeping all operations **O(log n)** in the worst case.

### Insertion Strategy

1. Insert the node as in a standard BST.
2. Color the new node **red** (this preserves property 5 cheaply).
3. **Fix up** any violations of properties 2 or 4 by walking up the tree.

### Fix-Up Cases (after inserting node `z`):

```
function RB_Insert_Fixup(tree, z):
    while z.parent.color == RED:
        if z.parent == z.parent.parent.left:
            uncle = z.parent.parent.right

            // Case 1: Uncle is RED → recolor
            if uncle.color == RED:
                z.parent.color = BLACK
                uncle.color = BLACK
                z.parent.parent.color = RED
                z = z.parent.parent          // move up two levels

            else:
                // Case 2: z is a right child → left rotate to reduce to Case 3
                if z == z.parent.right:
                    z = z.parent
                    Left_Rotate(tree, z)

                // Case 3: z is a left child → recolor + right rotate
                z.parent.color = BLACK
                z.parent.parent.color = RED
                Right_Rotate(tree, z.parent.parent)

        else:
            // Mirror: swap "left" and "right" in all of the above

    tree.root.color = BLACK                  // enforce property 2
```

### Rotation Pseudocode

```
function Left_Rotate(tree, x):
    y = x.right
    x.right = y.left            // turn y's left subtree into x's right subtree
    if y.left ≠ NULL:
        y.left.parent = x
    y.parent = x.parent          // link x's parent to y
    if x.parent == NULL:
        tree.root = y
    else if x == x.parent.left:
        x.parent.left = y
    else:
        x.parent.right = y
    y.left = x                   // put x on y's left
    x.parent = y
```

`Right_Rotate` is the symmetric mirror.

### Why It Works

| Mechanism | What it fixes |
|---|---|
| **Recoloring** (Case 1) | Pushes the red-red violation upward without changing structure. Runs O(log n) times at most. |
| **Rotation** (Cases 2 & 3) | Restructures locally (1–2 rotations max) to restore the no-consecutive-reds property. |

Because at most **2 rotations** are needed per insertion (and recoloring is O(log n)), insertion is **O(log n)** worst-case — unlike a plain BST's O(n).

### Complexity Summary

| Operation | Plain BST (worst) | Red-Black Tree (worst) |
|---|---|---|
| Insert | O(n) | O(log n) |
| Search | O(n) | O(log n) |
| Delete | O(n) | O(log n) |
| Rotations per insert | N/A | ≤ 2 |
| Recolors per insert | N/A | O(log n) |
